description: Apollo 10 Flight Journal - Day 6, part 27: Tracking, snapping and napping

tokens: Having had a very busy day with the LM solo operations, and rendezvous and docking, the crew were offered the opportunity of an extended sleep period.
However they awoke at approximately the original wakeup time in the Flight Plan, at 116:30 GET.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The final day in lunar orbit for Apollo 10 was to be rather long, with TEI due at 137:36:28.9 GET and the next sleep period commencing at 140:30 GET, a full 24 hours after they woke up.
This was also a busy period of landmark tracking, photography, TV transmissions and preparation for TEI.
It was therefore deemed necessary in the planning of the flight to build in a short rest (nap) period of approximately 3½ hours, terminating 3 revolutions before the TEI manoeuvre.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 127:22:--Begin lunar Rev-27
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-81.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The crew are discussing the events that occurred at LM staging.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The crew are now marking again on landmark CP-1.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young can just see the landmark CP-2 coming into the telescope field of view.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The crew is now marking again on landmark CP-2.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Hasselblad that failed whilst in use in the LM, has been carried back into the CSM, put to use again, but is continuing to only work intermittently.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-82.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hello.
Apollo 10, Houston.
We're standing by.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger; we're right in the middle of the track on [garble].
We'll call you later.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Crew now marking on landmark F-1.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 10, Houston.
We got the data.
You can proceed.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Landing site 3 in Sinus Medii.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Gashes are a group of smallish craters in southern Mare Tranquillitatis that lead into the crater Moltke, not far from landing site 2 and landmark 130.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crew now marking again on landmark 130.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: OK, Houston.
We've finished with landmark 130.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger, 10.
We copy.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: 10, Houston.
We've got quite a few things to talk to you about on the Flight Plan.
We'd like you to, for your rest attitude, to change your roll angle from 180 to 090.
Rest of it's OK.
Your High Gain changes to a pitch of 00 and a yaw of 240.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: OK, Charlie.
Why don't you run us by that again.
Nobody was on the headset.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: OK.
Roger.
We've got the data.
You can proceed on out of 22 [program 22], and we got the Flight Plan updates for you starting on page 3-82.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger.
Wait a second.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Go ahead, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: OK.
On the rest attitude at 128:29, we'd like you to maneuver to a roll of 090 so we can cool down quad A, and the pitch and yaw are the same as listed in the Flight Plan.
The High Gain Antenna angles are pitch of 00, yaw 240.
OK.
Go on down the page to fuel cell O2purge - we'd like you to do the fuel cell on O2purge on fuel cells 2 and 3.
And, same page, we have a map update Rev-29, if you are ready to copy.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Go ahead, and I am going to go ahead and start the fuel - the O2purge right now on 2 and 3.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Only fuel cells 2 and 3 are being purged, as fuel cell 1 has been open circuited, taken off the buses due to the issue with the pump package.
It will be brought on line again briefly for the TEI manoeuvre.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
OK.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Do you want to read that back to me.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: OK, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Rev-29 map update details are as follows:LOS:131:12:24Crossing 150°W:131:23:18AOS:131:58:35.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
And let's go on to page 3-85 in the Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: OK.
By the way, we changed canister B a little bit late.
Let me find it for you.
I'll tell you exactly when we changed it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Standing by.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We changed it at 120 - We changed canister B late.
We changed it at 127 hours.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: OK.
We copy canister B at 127 hours, and we have an update on page 3-85 if you're ready to copy.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Go ahead, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: OK, Gene.
3-85, the update for the oblique strip - we're giving you the same update as this morning.
Roll, pitch and yaw are; roll, 180, 339, 000.
Your T0f:8 time is 132:17:48, change to f:2.8 at 132:27:16.
OK.
Your T1time is 132:30:16.
Now the f:8 and the f:2.8 f-stops are - stop changes are for the sequence camera only.
We'd like you to use f:4 at 1/125 for the black and white.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: OK.
I got oblique strip; roll - roll, 180; pitch, 339; yaw, 000.
T0is at 132:17:48 - that's at a f:8 and go to f:2 [means f:2.8] at 132:27:16; and that's all under sequence [camera].
T2is at 132:32:16 and use f:4 1/125 on the black and white all the way.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The oblique photography is of the approach to landing site 3 in Sinus Medii.

The PAD breaks down as:Attitude for photography:Roll, 180°; Pitch, 339°; Yaw 000°T0, the time to start the photography at an aperture of f:8:132:17:48Stopping down to f:2.8:132:27:16T1, the time to start pitching down at 0.5°/sec:132:30:16T2, the time the pitch down rate is stopped:132:32:16Following T1, the exposure should be f:4 at 1/125 sec.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: OK, Gene-o.
Good readback.
Those roll, pitch and yaw angles are ORDEAL angles.
OK, I've got a T - TEI-29 if you're ready to copy.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: OK.
Before we start on the PAD, we'd like you P00 and Accept.
We have a state vector for you.
And coming with a TEI-29, SPS G&N: start with Noun 33, 133:37:36.69; plus 34079, plus 00166, plus 01719; pitch, 056; two jets for 14 seconds on the ullage.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie.
Give me Noun 81 Delta-VXagain, will you please?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Which Delta-V you want, Gene?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Delta-VX.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: OK.
Delta-VXis plus 34079.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: OK.
TEI-29, SPS and G&N: Noun 33 is 133:37:36.69; Noun 81 is plus 34079, plus 00166 plus 01719; and pitch is 056.
It's two jets ullage for 14 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The TEI 29 PAD is interpreted as follows:Purpose:The PAD is a revised version for an emergency burn to return to Earth at the end of Rev-29.Systems:The burn would be made using the SPS engine, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.Vehicle weight (Noun 47):NAPitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):NATime of ignition (Noun 33):133 hours, 37 minutes, 36.69 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):X, +3,407.9 (+1,038.7); Y, +16.6 (+5.1); Z, +171.9 (+52.4).

The large positive number in the X direction implies a large prograde component, essentially adding to their orbital velocity, exactly what would be expected from an escape manoeuvre.Spacecraft attitude (IMU angles):Roll, NA; Pitch, 56°; Yaw, NA.Additional notes are that two RCS jets are burned for 14 seconds for ullage.
All the subsequent items on the form are not applicable to this manoeuvre.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
That's a good readback.
Now, I've got some stuff for you on the fuel cells and the cryos wed like for your sleep period.
Its quite lengthy.
You might have a pencil ready so you can copy down some of it, and Ill try to read it slowly and repeat all that you need.
We'd like you to stir the cryos and - before going to sleep - and then turn all the fans off.
We'd like optics power switch, Off, SCS Electronics to ECA.
O2tank 2 heater, Auto, O2tank 1 heater to Off.
Are you with me?
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'm with you, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: OK, Gene.
Continuing on.
We'd like H2tank 1 heater, Auto, H2tank heater to Off.
You can leave battery B on Charge, and we'd like to power up the High Gain Antenna for the sleep.
On the fuel cell configuration, you can leave as is, and we'll place fuel cell 1 on both Main A and Main B one and a half hours prior to the TEI burn.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: OK, Charlie.
I understand.
I'll cycle the Cryo fans and then I'll [garble] optics power, Off.
SCS electronic, ECA.
O2tank heater number 2 to Auto; number 1 to Off; H2tank 1 to Auto, and 2 Off.
Continue Batt B Charge, and I'll power up to the High Gain.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: With the crew sleeping, MCC-H need the High Gain Antenna powered up, so that high bit rate data is available to allow them to remotely monitor the CSM systems.
The battery B charge can continue during the rest period as the load overall load during this time will be reduced.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger, Gene.
Good readback, Now that - at our present temperature decrease on fuel cell 1, we will not have to put it on the line prior to the above time.
The minimum temperature for fuel cell 1 is now 370 degrees - that's 370 skin temp.
So we'll place it on the main buses to raise the temp in lieu of using the inline heaters during trans-Earth coast.
I'd like to emphasize, please do not use the fuel cell heater on fuel cell 1.
And, at the present rate of decay of temperature in fuel cell l, it looks like we'll have to put it on the buses about every 20 hours.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: By placing fuel cell 1 on-line periodically, the load on it will raise the fuel cell skin temperature and allow some control over that temperature.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Fine.
That suits us fine, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: OK.
Good.
One little last thing is that we got some word - some word for you on the storage of the LM cameras and the LiOH canister.
We'd like - we suggest that you wrap the cameras in an LCG and store them in A-8.
Stand by one.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Stand by.
We got a recent update on the camera stowage.
We'll be right with you.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger.
Houston.
Say again, now.
We - We're going to go to the - maneuver to the sleep attitude at this time.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
That's fine, Tom.
Go ahead.
Go ahead for the maneuver, and your computer is yours.
And I'll be back with you when we get to High Gain on the Hasselblad and canister.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: OK.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, Apollo 10.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Go ahead, 10.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger.
Let me summarize this landmark tracking thing, the last pass.
I think we only got four marks on one of those CPs.
And - and let me summarize the whole thing by saying, I think you ought to throw out CP-1's first pass and F-l's first pass because I don't think they were on them - they were on the same site.
I don't think CP-1 was the same site that actual CP-1 was on the other three passes.
Same way for F-1's first pass.
But I feel pretty good about all the rest of the sightings.
I think they're all on the same spot of ground, and I'll point it out to you when we get back.
And I guarantee you that all the stuff on 130 was on 130.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger, John.
We cop., It was really a great show on the landmark tracking.
Everybody's worked real - very pleased with everything, and we understand you suggest throwing out the first pass of CP-1 and F-1 due to incorrect - possible incorrect landmarks.
On the last pass of CP-1, we copy - we're only four marks on that one, but they look real good.
And everybody in MPAD is happy as a clam with everything, and we're all set for you guys to go to sleep.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: MPAD is the Mission Planning and Analysis Division, part of the Flight Operations Directorate.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: OK.
And if we don't learn nothing else, we ought to know where 130 is.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Hey, John.
On - On 130 you're within 300 feet [90 meters] on your altitudes on every pass across 130.
It's really great.
The MPAD's calling you the mechanical man.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I was just tracking that [garble].
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Well, you should have seen him.
He was really putting it right on the money there.
It was great.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
We could tell that from the data, Tom.
It 1ooks really great.
10, that's all we got for you.
We're going to hold up on...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: ...[garble].
Yes, I was going to say, Charlie, if George Mueller is around there, tell him to smile.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: George Mueller is Associate Administrator of the NASA Office of Manned Space Flight.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: OK.
He was grinning last night.
I haven't seen him tonight.
That's all we got for you, 10.
We're going to hold up on this stowage of the cameras and the canister until we come back in the trans-Earth coast.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yes.
Roger.
We're only going to be pulling about one g when the engine fires off here, Charlie.
We got everything already pretty much configured for that, so we'll be in good shape to take care of the rest of it.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Stafford is referring to the SPS engine's TEI burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
We concur.
Out.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Hello, 10.
We have you on the High Gain now.
Do you read me?
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger, Charlie.
Read you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger, Tom.
One further recommendation.
Quads Bravo and Charlie are the fattest, and we recommend for the sleep period you configure the DAP for attitude control using quads Bravo and Charlie.
And otherwise you're in good shape.
Good night, and we'll see you in 3½ hours or so.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: OK.
Roger.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: 128:50:XX PAO:This is Apollo Control at 128 hours, 50 minutes.
The Apollo 10 crew begins a 3½-hour rest period after a considerable amount of landmark tracking and photography today.
We still have 23 minutes acquisition in this pass.
We will stay up through the Loss Of Signal, however, we probably will not hear from the crew or contact them again during this pass.
First half of the pass during this acquisition on the 27th revolution was very quiet as the crew completed landmark tracking on landmark 130.
We then passed up some Flight Plan updates.
Gave them procedure for the fuel cells during the rest period and informed them that we'd like to have fuel cell 1, the fuel cell that has been...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, Apollo 10.
Again on these quads, you want us - we don't want to use quad A.
You want us to use quads B and D?
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: That's negative.
Quads Bravo and Charlie.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger.
You want us to fail A and Dog.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Affirmative.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Fuel cell 1 has been off the lines since a pump associated with the fuel cell failed some time ago.
The fuel cell itself is all right.
There's nothing wrong with the fuel cell and that fuel cell 1 will be placed back on the line an hour and a half prior to the trans-Earth injection burn.
John Young again reported on the tracking tasks and received praise from the ground for the job he has been doing in this landmark tracking.
We'll continue to stay up through - live through LOS about 20 minutes from now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hello, Apollo 10, Houston.
Sorry to disturb you again, but we notice your DAP quad fails incorrectly.
We'd like you to load 00110 so that well have some pitch attitude control.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: OK, you want 0 - Say that again, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger, Tom.
In register 2, we'd like 00110.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The DSKY register 2 DAP code breaks down as:0 = Use quadrants A & C for roll control.0 = Quadrant A failed (OFF).1 = Quadrant B operational (ON).1 = Quadrant C operational (ON).0 = Quadrant D failed (OFF).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: OK.
You got it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Hello, Apollo 10.
Houston.
We're not going to give up.
We got - we noticed your quad Charlie Auto RCS SELECT switch is Off.
We'd like for you to configure Bravo and Charlie Auto RCS Select switches, On.
Alpha and Dog, Off.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger.
Alpha and Dog, Off; Bravo and Charlie, On.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The Auto RCS Select switch for the active SM RCS quadrants, on panel 8, must be in the On position to allow the DAP to control their firing.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Affirmative.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Hey, maybe it's late at night, Charlie, but you want Alpha and Charlie, Off; Bravo and Dog, On.
How you going to get any pitch control?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We want Bravo and Charlie, On, and Alpha and Delta, Off.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Alrighty.
Bravo and Charlie, On; Alpha and Delta, Off.
Got it.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 129 hours, 13 minutes.
We've had Loss Of Signal on revolution 27.
Acquisition time for revolution 28 is 130 hours.
We do not expect to have the conversation with the crew during that acquisition.
We're now in a 3½-hour rest period.
We will come up and bring you any conversation we do have on the next revolution.
We're now showing Apollo 10 in a 67.9 by 53.8 nautical mile (125.7 x 99.6 km) orbit with an orbital period of 1 hour, 58 minutes, 50 seconds.
This is Mission Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 129:18:--Begin lunar Rev-28
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-83.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-84.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 130 hours, 5 minutes.
We have acquired Apollo 10 on the 28 revolution.
There has been no conversation, and we do not expect any.
The crew still has an hour and 52 minutes in its rest period.
The TEI time, trans-Earth injection time has not changed yet, still remaining 137:36:28.
This may be refined later, but the present time, that is the latest ignition time.
The black team which has been led by Gerry Griffin today will change shifts slightly early this evening, and we have scheduled the change of shift news conference for 10:30 pm Central Daylight Time.
If there is conversation during this acquisition time, we'll bring that to you.
This is Mission Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 130 hours, 15 minutes.
Flight surgeon reports that Tom Stafford's heart rate is in the mid-forties, that coupled with his respiration rate indicates that he sleeping.
Stafford is the only crew member being monitored at this time.
John Young and Gene Cernan are in the sleep stations beneath the couches and do not have biomed monitoring at this time.
This is Mission Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 131:15:--Begin lunar Rev-29
transcript.speaker: Commentary
